While blocking calcium channels would indeed reduce ACh release by preventing Ca²⁺ influx needed for vesicle fusion, blocking potassium (K⁺) channels actually has the opposite effect; it can lead to more sustained depolarization, thereby increasing the likelihood of action potential firing and thus enhancing ACh release. Additionally, while chloride channels' primary role is modulating postsynaptic responses, their blockage indirectly supports increased excitability through changes in intracellular chloride concentrations, contributing to higher neurotransmitter release.
